Introduction to Python Project : FoodHub Data Analysis¶

Problem Statement¶

The rapid growth in the number of restaurants in New York, combined with the busy lifestyles of students and working professionals, has led to an increased dependence on online food delivery services. FoodHub, a food aggregator platform, provides customers with convenient access to multiple restaurants through a single smartphone application while enabling restaurants to receive and fulfill online orders efficiently.

Despite the convenience offered, managing order fulfillment, delivery efficiency, customer satisfaction, and restaurant performance remains a significant challenge for FoodHub. The platform generates large volumes of data related to customer orders, restaurant operations, delivery times, order costs, and customer ratings. However, without proper analysis, this data cannot be effectively utilized to improve operational efficiency, enhance customer experience, or optimize service quality.

Therefore, there is a need to analyze FoodHub’s order data to understand customer behavior, restaurant performance, and delivery dynamics in order to support data-driven decision-making and improve overall platform performance.

Objectives¶

The food aggregator company has stored the data of the different orders made by the registered customers in their online portal. They want to analyze the data to get a fair idea about the demand of different restaurants which will help them in enhancing their customer experience. Suppose you are hired as a Data Scientist in this company and the Data Science team has shared some of the key questions that need to be answered. Perform the data analysis to find answers to these questions that will help the company improve its business.

Data Dictionary¶

  1. order_id: Unique identifier for each order.

  2. customer_id: Unique ID of the customer placing the order.

  3. restaurant_name: Name of the restaurant from which the order was placed.

  4. cuisine_type: Type of cuisine ordered.

  5. cost_of_the_order: Total cost of the food order.

  6. day_of_the_week: Indicates whether the order was placed on a weekday or weekend.

  7. rating: Customer rating for the order (out of 5).

  8. food_preparation_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the restaurant to prepare the food.

  9. delivery_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the delivery partner to deliver the order.

Let us start by importing the required libraries¶

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

Understanding the structure of the data¶

# Mount Google Drive

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

Univariate Analysis¶

Question 6: Explore all the variables and provide observations on their distributions. (Generally, histograms, boxplots, countplots, etc. are used for univariate exploration.) [9 marks]¶

In [ ]:
# # Numerical variables that are significant
#univariate Analysis for Numerical data
num_vars = ['cost_of_the_order', 'food_preparation_time', 'delivery_time']

for col in num_vars:
    plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
    sns.histplot(df[col], kde=True)
    plt.title(f'Distribution of {col}')
    plt.show()

    sns.boxplot(x=df[col])
    plt.title(f'Boxplot of {col}')
    plt.show()



#Univariate analysis for categorical data
num_vars = ['rating', 'cuisine_type', 'day_of_the_week','restaurant_name']
for col in num_vars:
    df[col].value_counts().head(10).plot(kind='barh')
    plt.title(f'Top 10 Restaurants:{col}')
    plt.show()

Multivariate Analysis¶

Question 12: Perform a multivariate analysis to explore relationships between the important variables in the dataset. (It is a good idea to explore relations between numerical variables as well as relations between numerical and categorical variables) [10 marks]¶

In [ ]:
#Multivariate analysis
#Numerical Relationships
#1.Food preparation time vs delivery time

sns.set(style='whitegrid')
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.lineplot(data=df, x=df['food_preparation_time'], y=df['delivery_time'])
plt.title('food_preparation_time Vs delivery_time')
plt.xlabel('food_preparation_time')
plt.ylabel('delivery_time')
plt.show()


#2. Scatter Plot: Cost of Order vs Delivery Time
sns.scatterplot(data=df, x='cost_of_the_order', y='delivery_time')
plt.title("Cost of Order vs Delivery Time")
plt.xlabel("Cost of Order")
plt.ylabel("Delivery Time")
plt.show()


#3. Correlation Heatmap of Numerical Variables
import numpy as np
numerical_cols = ['cost_of_the_order', 'rating', 'food_preparation_time', 'delivery_time']
df[numerical_cols] = df[numerical_cols].replace("Not given", np.nan)
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.heatmap(df[numerical_cols].corr(), annot=True, cmap='coolwarm')
plt.title("Correlation Heatmap (Numerical Variables)")
plt.show()



#Numerical Vs Categorical Analysis
#4.Cuisine Type vs Cost of Order (Boxplot)
plt.figure(figsize=(8,4))
sns.boxplot(x='cuisine_type', y='cost_of_the_order', data=df)
plt.title('Cuisine Type vs Cost of Order')
plt.xlabel('Cuisine Type')
plt.ylabel('Cost of Order')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()


#5.Day of Week vs Delivery Time (Boxplot)
plt.figure(figsize=(8,4))
sns.boxplot(x='day_of_the_week', y='delivery_time', data=df)
plt.title('Day of Week vs Delivery Time')
plt.xlabel('Day of Week')
plt.ylabel('Delivery Time')
plt.show()

#6.Restaurant Name vs Rating (Barplot)
df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(df['rating'], errors='coerce')
df_clean = df.dropna(subset=['rating'])
top_restaurants = df_clean.groupby('restaurant_name')['rating'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False).head(10)
plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
sns.barplot(x=top_restaurants.index, y=top_restaurants.values)
plt.title('Top Restaurants vs Average Rating')
plt.xlabel('Restaurant Name')
plt.ylabel('Average Rating')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()



#7. cat vs cat relationship
#Cuisine Type vs Day of Week (Heatmap / Crosstab)
# Create crosstab (counts for each cuisine type per day)
crosstab = pd.crosstab(df['cuisine_type'], df['day_of_the_week'])
plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))
sns.heatmap(crosstab, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Blues')
plt.title('Cuisine Type vs Day of Week')
plt.xlabel('Day of Week')
plt.ylabel('Cuisine Type')
plt.show()





#8.Customer ID vs Cuisine Type
# Countplot for Top 5 Frequent Customers
df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(df['rating'], errors='coerce')
top_restaurants = df.groupby('restaurant_name')['rating'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False).head(10)
plt.figure(figsize=(8,4))
sns.barplot(x=top_restaurants.index, y=top_restaurants.values)
plt.title('Top Restaurants vs Average Rating')
plt.xlabel('Restaurant Name')
plt.ylabel('Average Rating')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

Question 13: The company wants to provide a promotional offer in the advertisement of the restaurants. The condition to get the offer is that the restaurants must have a rating count of more than 50 and the average rating should be greater than 4. Find the restaurants fulfilling the criteria to get the promotional offer. [3 marks]¶

In [ ]:
df['rating'] = pd.to_numeric(df['rating'], errors='coerce')
df = df.dropna(subset=['rating'])
restaurant_stats = df.groupby('restaurant_name')['rating'].agg(
    rating_count='count',
    average_rating='mean'
)

eligible_restaurants = restaurant_stats[
    (restaurant_stats['rating_count'] > 50) &
    (restaurant_stats['average_rating'] > 4)
]


print(eligible_restaurants)

                           rating_count  average_rating
restaurant_name                                        
Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken            64        4.328125
Blue Ribbon Sushi                    73        4.219178
Shake Shack                         133        4.278195
The Meatball Shop                    84        4.511905

Observations:¶

Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken ,Blue Ribbon Sushi,Shake Shack and The Meatball Shop are the restaurants that have a rating count of more than 50 and the average rating should be greater than 4. These restaurants fulfill the criteria to get the promotional offer.

Question 14: The company charges the restaurant 25% on the orders having cost greater than 20 dollars and 15% on the orders having cost greater than 5 dollars. Find the net revenue generated by the company across all orders. [3 marks]¶

In [ ]:
df['cost_of_the_order'] = pd.to_numeric(df['cost_of_the_order'], errors='coerce')
df['revenue'] = 0
df.loc[df['cost_of_the_order'] > 20, 'revenue'] = df['cost_of_the_order'] * 0.25
df.loc[(df['cost_of_the_order'] > 5) & (df['cost_of_the_order'] <= 20), 'revenue'] = df['cost_of_the_order'] * 0.15

# Calculate total revenue
total_revenue = df['revenue'].sum()

print("Net revenue generated by the company:", total_revenue)

Net revenue generated by the company: 3865.572

  df.loc[df['cost_of_the_order'] > 20, 'revenue'] = df['cost_of_the_order'] * 0.25

Question 15: The company wants to analyze the total time required to deliver the food. What percentage of orders take more than 60 minutes to get delivered from the time the order is placed? (The food has to be prepared and then delivered.) [2 marks]¶

In [ ]:
df['food_preparation_time'] = pd.to_numeric(df['food_preparation_time'], errors='coerce')
df['delivery_time'] = pd.to_numeric(df['delivery_time'], errors='coerce')

df = df.dropna(subset=['food_preparation_time', 'delivery_time'])

df['total_time'] = df['food_preparation_time'] + df['delivery_time']
total_orders = len(df)
late_orders = len(df[df['total_time'] > 60])

percentage = (late_orders / total_orders) * 100

print("Percentage of orders taking more than 60 minutes:", round(percentage, 2), "%")

Percentage of orders taking more than 60 minutes: 10.24 %

  df['delivery_time'] = pd.to_numeric(df['delivery_time'], errors='coerce')

Observations:¶

It has been found that 10.24% of orders are taking more than 60 minutes. Most of the orders take less than 60 minutes.

Question 16: The company wants to analyze the delivery time of the orders on weekdays and weekends. How does the mean delivery time vary during weekdays and weekends? [2 marks]¶

In [ ]:
df['delivery_time'] = pd.to_numeric(df['delivery_time'], errors='coerce')
df = df.dropna(subset=['delivery_time'])

# Calculate mean delivery time
mean_delivery = df.groupby('day_of_the_week')['delivery_time'].mean()

print(mean_delivery)

day_of_the_week
Weekday    28.305882
Weekend    22.436740
Name: delivery_time, dtype: float64

Observations:¶

The delivery time changes by 5.869142 from weekday to weekend. On weekends delivery time is less than weekdays.

Conclusion and Recommendations¶

Question 17: What are your conclusions from the analysis? What recommendations would you like to share to help improve the business? (You can use cuisine type and feedback ratings to drive your business recommendations.) [6 marks]¶

Conclusions:¶

Restaurants with higher average ratings and a large number of reviews perform consistently better.Only a limited number of restaurants meet both conditions of high rating count (>50) and average rating (>4), indicating quality differentiation.

Most orders are delivered within a reasonable time; however, a significant percentage of orders take more than 60 minutes when food preparation time is high.

Weekends show higher average delivery time compared to weekdays due to increased order volume.

Certain cuisines like american, chineese etc receive higher ratings and more frequent orders, showing strong customer preference. Cuisine demand varies by day of the week, with some cuisines being more popular on weekends.

Orders with higher cost (>20 dollars) contribute more to company revenue due to higher commission rates. Lower-cost orders generate less revenue individually but help maintain order volume.

Frequent customers tend to order from specific restaurants and cuisines, indicating stable preferences and loyalty.

Increase delivery resources during weekdays. Work with restaurants to reduce food preparation time to improve overall delivery speed.

Orders with higher cost (>20 dollars) contribute more to company revenue due to higher commission rates. Lower-cost orders generate less revenue individually but help maintain order volume.

Positive and good relationship shown between cost_of_the_order and rating, cost_of_the_order and food_preparation_time.

Recommendations:¶

Promote restaurants that have high average ratings and sufficient rating count to increase customer trust. Use these restaurants in advertisements and featured listings.

Increase delivery resources during weekdays. Work with restaurants to reduce food preparation time to improve overall delivery speed.

Launch weekend offers for popular cuisines.Provide discounts or promotions for less popular cuisines to balance demand.

Encourage higher order value through combo meals and upselling strategies. Focus marketing efforts on high-value customers and premium restaurants.

Analyze feedback and ratings to improve low-performing restaurants. Introduce loyalty programs for frequent customers to increase retention.
